---
title: "Extra Lecture — Pseudobulk Differential Expression"
author: "Bill Cresko"
format:
  revealjs:
    footer: Single Cell RNA-seq Workshop
    transition: fade
    transition-speed: slow
    theme: default
    slide-number: true
    code-fold: false
    code-overflow: wrap
    code-line-numbers: true
    highlight-style: github
    embed-resources: true
    css: cresko_style.css
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
editor: visual
---

```{r}
#| label: setup
#| include: false

library(tidyverse)
library(knitr)

# Referenced but not evaluated in these slides:
# library(Seurat)
# library(DESeq2)
# library(edgeR)
# library(limma)
# library(glmGamPoi)

theme_set(theme_minimal(base_size = 14))
set.seed(2026)
```

# Pseudobulk Differential Expression {background-color="#2c3e50"}

## Goals of this lecture

::: incremental
-   Understand why per-cell DE between **conditions** is often statistically wrong
-   See how pseudobulk fixes it
-   Run a pseudobulk workflow in Seurat + DESeq2
-   Know when pseudobulk is and is not the right tool
:::

# Part 1 — The problem with per-cell DE {background-color="#2c3e50"}

## The scientific question

> In *cell type X*, which genes differ between **treated** and **control** subjects?

## Why per-cell tests go wrong

::: incremental
-   Cells from one subject are **not independent observations** — they share genetics, library prep, batch, treatment
-   Treating each cell as an independent replicate inflates the effective N by 100×–10,000×
-   Result: **anti-conservative p-values** — thousands of spuriously "significant" genes
-   This has been shown repeatedly in benchmarks (Squair et al. 2021; Zimmerman et al. 2021)
:::

::: callout-important
If your experimental unit is the **subject**, your statistical unit should be the **subject**.
:::

## The fix in one sentence

> **Aggregate counts per (sample, cell type); run DESeq2 / edgeR / limma-voom on the result.**

# Part 2 — Pseudobulk, visually {background-color="#2c3e50"}

## What pseudobulk does

![](images/pseudobulk_concept.svg){fig-align="center" width="95%"}

## What changes vs. bulk RNA-seq?

::: incremental
-   Structurally — **nothing**. The pseudobulk matrix looks like a bulk counts matrix
-   You use the **same tools**: DESeq2, edgeR, limma-voom
-   The only differences are:
    -   One matrix **per cell type**
    -   Samples with too few cells in that type get dropped or flagged
:::

# Part 3 — Doing it in Seurat + DESeq2 {background-color="#2c3e50"}

## Build the pseudobulk matrix

``` r
library(Seurat)

# seu is an integrated, annotated Seurat object with:
#   seu$sample    — biological sample ID (subject / replicate)
#   seu$condition — treatment vs control
#   seu$celltype  — annotated cell type

pb <- AggregateExpression(
  seu,
  assays    = "RNA",
  group.by  = c("celltype", "sample"),
  return.seurat = FALSE
)$RNA
```

`pb` is a genes × (celltype_sample) count matrix — ready for DESeq2.

## Fit DESeq2 per cell type

``` r
library(DESeq2)

run_pb_de <- function(ct, pb_mat, sample_meta) {
  cols <- grep(paste0("^", ct, "_"), colnames(pb_mat), value = TRUE)
  mat  <- pb_mat[, cols]
  meta <- sample_meta[match(sub(paste0("^", ct, "_"), "", cols),
                            sample_meta$sample), ]

  dds <- DESeqDataSetFromMatrix(countData = mat,
                                colData   = meta,
                                design    = ~ condition)
  dds <- DESeq(dds)
  results(dds, contrast = c("condition", "treated", "control")) |> as.data.frame()
}

res_by_ct <- lapply(unique(seu$celltype),
                    run_pb_de, pb_mat = pb, sample_meta = sample_meta)
names(res_by_ct) <- unique(seu$celltype)
```

## edgeR and limma-voom alternatives

``` r
library(edgeR)
y <- DGEList(counts = mat, group = meta$condition)
y <- calcNormFactors(y)
design <- model.matrix(~ condition, meta)
y <- estimateDisp(y, design)
fit <- glmQLFit(y, design)
qlf <- glmQLFTest(fit, coef = 2)
topTags(qlf)
```

``` r
library(limma)
v   <- voom(y, design, plot = FALSE)
fit <- lmFit(v, design) |> eBayes()
topTable(fit, coef = 2, number = Inf)
```

::: callout-tip
All three give well-calibrated inference. DESeq2 is the most common default in the scRNA-seq literature.
:::

## Python alternative — decoupleR + PyDESeq2

``` python
import decoupler as dc
import pydeseq2.ds as ds

pb = dc.get_pseudobulk(adata, sample_col="sample",
                       groups_col="celltype", min_cells=10, min_counts=1000)

# Then fit PyDESeq2 per celltype, contrast=treated vs control
```

# Part 4 — Practical guidance {background-color="#2c3e50"}

## When to use pseudobulk

::: incremental
-   Comparing **conditions across biological samples** (treatment, genotype, time)
-   You have at least \~3 samples per group (ideally 5+)
-   Cell type of interest has enough cells per sample (a common rule: \~10+ cells)
:::

## When **not** to use pseudobulk

::: incremental
-   Finding **cluster markers** for annotation — use within-cell methods (`FindAllMarkers`, Wilcoxon, MAST)
-   Only 1–2 samples per condition — no replication to pseudobulk over
-   Continuous traits measured per cell (e.g. pseudotime) — use models that handle the continuous covariate
:::

## Common pitfalls

::: incremental
-   **Too few cells per sample** per cell type → zero-inflated pseudobulk; drop that cell type or those samples with a `min_cells` filter
-   **Confounded designs**: if every treated sample is on one 10x run, integration won't rescue bad design
-   **Ignoring covariates**: include `batch`, `sex`, `age` in the design formula — DESeq2 will use them
-   **Reporting single-cell N**: always report N = number of **samples**, not number of cells, for condition-level DE
:::

## Diagnostic plots worth running

::: incremental
-   PCA of the pseudobulk samples **within a cell type** — do replicates cluster by condition, or by batch?
-   MA / volcano plots per cell type
-   Hierarchical clustering of pseudobulk samples — quick confound check
:::

## Summary

::: incremental
-   Per-cell DE between conditions → **anti-conservative p-values**
-   Pseudobulk recovers proper replication and lets you use battle-tested bulk tools
-   Keep per-cell methods for **marker genes within a dataset**, use pseudobulk for **condition comparisons across subjects**
:::

## Further reading

-   Squair *et al.* 2021, *Nature Communications* — "Confronting false discoveries in single-cell differential expression"
-   Zimmerman *et al.* 2021, *Nature Communications* — "A practical solution to pseudoreplication bias in single-cell studies"
-   Crowell *et al.* 2020, *Nature Communications* — muscat, a pseudobulk toolkit for scRNA-seq
-   Heumos *et al.* 2023 — "Best practices for single-cell analysis across modalities"
